subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 04, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Volume-controlled mechanical ventilation during cardiopulmonar
sentences: Story 1 is about Volume-controlled mechanical ventilation during cardiopulmonary resuscitation: A systematic review and meta-analysis..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from Resuscitation.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in children or adults with cardiac arrest and an advanced airway were included, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
We screened 12,004 abstracts and included 13 articles consisting of six trials enrolling 461 patients and seven non-randomized studies including 4,607 patients.

Meta-analysis of 120 patients from two randomized trials comparing volume-controlled to manual ventilation showed no difference for return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) (odds ratio (OR), 1.31; 95%-confidence interval (CI), 0.64 to 2.71).
Non-randomized studies were at serious or critical risk of bias.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Intensive care admissions for severe immune-related adverse ev
sentences: Story 2 is about Intensive care admissions for severe immune-related adverse events associated with immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy: a 10-year cohort study..
This is a cohort study from Journal of critical care.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients admitted to the Lille University Hospital ICU for ICI-related irAEs between January 2015 and May 2024, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Among 244 ICI-treated patients admitted to the ICU, 55 (23%) presented an irAE.
The most frequent irAEs were myositis/myocarditis (29%) and pneumonitis (22%).
Median time from first ICI infusion to irAE onset was 63 days [IQR 21-144].
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Gastric Residual Volume Assessment in Critically Ill Children:
sentences: Story 3 is about Gastric Residual Volume Assessment in Critically Ill Children: The GASTRIC-PICU Randomized Clinical Trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from JAMA.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in intensive care units (PICUs) in the UK and 1 in Switzerland, how should we interpret s often lead to withholding feeds, impairing nutritional delivery. To evaluate the effect of not routinely assessing GRV compared with assessments at least every 6 hours in children undergoing mechanical ventilation on the duration of mechanical ventilati?

Of the 4700 children randomized (2352 to the no routine GRV assessment group and 2348 to the usual care group), 4460 were included in the intention-to-treat analysis (median [IQR] age, 8 [1-44] months; 1925 [42.6%] females).

No routine GRV assessment was noninferior to regular 6-hourly assessments for survival and days free from mechanical ventilation at 30 days (median [IQR], 25 [21-27] days in both groups; adjusted odds ratio [OR], 0.95 [95% CI, 0.86-1.05]).
Results of the per-protocol analysis were consistent with the intention-to-treat analysis (adjusted OR, 1.01 [95% CI, 0.90-1.13]).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.

The bedside implication is this: Potentially practice-relevant because a patient-centered outcome is reported; check absolute risk difference, adverse events, and whether your ICU population matches the study.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Volume-controlled mechanical ventilation during cardiopulmonary resuscitation: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.summary: To assess the evidence on volume-controlled mechanical ventilation versus manual ventilation or other ventilation modes during cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR). Randomized trials and non-randomized studies in children or adults with cardiac arrest and an advanced airway were included. We screened 12,004 abstracts and included 13 articles consisting of six trials enrolling 461 patients and seven non-randomized studies including 4,607 patients. Meta-analysis of 120 patients from two randomized trials comparing volume-controlled to manual ventilation showed no difference for return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) (odds ratio (OR), 1.31; 95%-confidence interval (CI), 0.64 to 2.71). Non-randomized studies were at serious or critical risk of bias.
articles.analysis: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.

pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: We screened 12,004 abstracts and included 13 articles consisting of six trials enrolling 461 patients and seven non-randomized studies including 4,607 patients. No study reported data on children. Meta-analysis of 120 patients from two randomized trials comparing volume-controlled to manual ventilation showed no difference for return of spontaneous...

keyResults: We screened 12,004 abstracts and included 13 articles consisting of six trials enrolling 461 patients and seven non-randomized studies including 4,607 patients.

Meta-analysis of 120 patients from two randomized trials comparing volume-controlled to manual ventilation showed no difference for return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) (odds ratio (OR), 1.31; 95%-confidence interval (CI), 0.64 to 2.71).
Non-randomized studies were at serious or critical risk of bias.
No study reported data on children.
criticalAppraisal: High-yield synthesis: check heterogeneity, included study quality, and whether ICU patients match your setting.
Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.
articles.practiceImpact: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.

articles.title: Intensive care admissions for severe immune-related adverse events associated with immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy: a 10-year cohort study.
articles.summary: Among 244 ICI-treated patients admitted to the ICU, 55 (23%) presented an irAE. The most frequent irAEs were myositis/myocarditis (29%) and pneumonitis (22%). Median time from first ICI infusion to irAE onset was 63 days [IQR 21-144]. Corticosteroids were initiated in 84% of cases, corresponding to 98% of treated patients; 39% were corticosteroid-refractory and 48% received additional immunosuppressive therapy. Half of the patients received vasopressors and 31% required mechanical ventilation.
articles.analysis: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.

pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.

Median time from first ICI infusion to irAE onset was 63 days [IQR 21-144]. Corticosteroids were initiated in 84% of cases, corresponding to 98% of treated patients; 39% were corticosteroid-...

keyResults: Among 244 ICI-treated patients admitted to the ICU, 55 (23%) presented an irAE.
The most frequent irAEs were myositis/myocarditis (29%) and pneumonitis (22%).
Median time from first ICI infusion to irAE onset was 63 days [IQR 21-144].
Corticosteroids were initiated in 84% of cases, corresponding to 98% of treated patients; 39% were corticosteroid-refractory and 48% received additional immunosuppressive therapy.
criticalAppraisal: Observational evidence: useful for association and prognosis, but residual confounding is likely.
Check confounding by indication, adjustment variables, immortal-time bias, and missing outcome data.
articles.practiceImpact: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.

articles.title: Gastric Residual Volume Assessment in Critically Ill Children: The GASTRIC-PICU Randomized Clinical Trial.
articles.summary: To evaluate the effect of not routinely assessing GRV compared with assessments at least every 6 hours in children undergoing mechanical ventilation on the duration of mechanical ventilation and survival and achievement of nutritional targets. Children were randomized (1:1) to receive usual care (GRV assessment every 6 hours) or no routine GRV assessment to guide enteral feeding. The clinical co-primary outcome (noninferiority) was a composite of survival and days free from mechanical ventilation at 30 days. The nutritional co-primary outcome (superiority) was the percentage of children meeting energy requirements by 72 hours. Of the 4700 children randomized (2352 to the no routine GRV assessment group and 2348 to the usual care group), 4460 were included in the intention-to-treat analysis (median [IQR] age, 8 [1-44] months; 1925 [42.6%] females).
articles.analysis: Potentially practice-relevant because a patient-centered outcome is reported; check absolute risk difference, adverse events, and whether your ICU population matches the study.

pico.population: intensive care units (PICUs) in the UK and 1 in Switzerland
pico.intervention: s often lead to withholding feeds, impairing nutritional delivery. To evaluate the effect of not routinely assessing GRV
pico.comparator: assessments at least every 6 hours in children undergoing mechanical ventilation on the duration of mechanical ventilati
pico.outcomes: The clinical co-primary outcome (noninferiority) was a composite of survival and days free from mechanical ventilation at 30 days. The nutritional co-primary outcome (superiority) was the percentage of children meeting energy requirements by 72 hours.

keyResults: Of the 4700 children randomized (2352 to the no routine GRV assessment group and 2348 to the usual care group), 4460 were included in the intention-to-treat analysis (median [IQR] age, 8 [1-44] months; 1925 [42.6%] females).

No routine GRV assessment was noninferior to regular 6-hourly assessments for survival and days free from mechanical ventilation at 30 days (median [IQR], 25 [21-27] days in both groups; adjusted odds ratio [OR], 0.95 [95% CI, 0.86-1.05]).
Results of the per-protocol analysis were consistent with the intention-to-treat analysis (adjusted OR, 1.01 [95% CI, 0.90-1.13]).

The mean percentage of energy requirements achieved by 72 hours was 80.3% in the no routine GRV assessment group and 76.8% in the usual care group (adjusted mean difference, 3.2 [95% CI, 1.3-5.2] percentage points; P < .001).
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.
articles.practiceImpact: Potentially practice-relevant because a patient-centered outcome is reported; check absolute risk difference, adverse events, and whether your ICU population matches the study.

I would interpret this cautiously because Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat..
